_note: Full node body text (markdown-ish, rendered pre-wrap). Merged onto nodes by id at load. Kept separate from pdd-graph.json so the structural model stays terse.
foundation.thesis:
Implementation capacity is cheap; expert attention is the bottleneck and rising in value.

So: size work to attention, and treat the acceptance set as the spec — not just its validation. In PDD the recovered characterization tests ARE the spec.
verb.map:
Recover ground truth from a system whose names lie. Read the code, not the schemas.

Deliverable: the System Truth Atlas — code map + footgun register + ranked risk register + silent-failure census, dated and anchored. The spec is recovered from the code, not written before it.
verb.pin:
Lock current behavior before you touch it.

Write characterization tests and a reproducibility baseline so any later change can be proven non-altering. The pinned tests are the recovered spec.
verb.carve: Cut the work into attention-sized units, and de-risk the risky cuts with a thin walking skeleton before committing the plan.
verb.ship: Make each change under mechanical gates — adequacy, no-silent-failures, properties, mutation grading — with root-cause discipline when runs don't converge.
verb.prove: Show behavior was preserved (replay/characterization), debt burned down, decisions reported, and hand over a go-forward Constitution so the gains don't rot.
pillar.constitution: Decided up front and applied thereafter, enforced by tools rather than memory. Here it covers tool discipline — run the checker, don't guess — and a deliberate protocol for when two rules deadlock.
pillar.trustworthy-green: Two failure modes hide behind a green suite: tests that pass without pinning behavior, and errors that vanish without a trace. The families here close both — adequacy, properties, and mutation grading make the tests catch a wrong change; keeping failures visible makes sure no error slips by unseen.
pillar.behavior-lock: Recovery (MAP) reads the system and writes the atlas; preservation proves no drift — replay if deterministic, golden-master, snapshot, or seam-injection if not. The technique varies; the obligation is constant.
family.adequacy: The behavior-pinning gates. Boundary cases, no-op branches, lifecycle and composition, value-pinning, invariant-exercisability, contract adequacy, negatives and edges, and mocking discipline — each one a way a test can pass without actually constraining the code.
family.nsf: The failure-visibility gates. Every error is handled or propagated, no blanket catches, no swallowed 'handled' errors, no orphan async tasks, subprocess results checked, and no test that turns a real defect into a pass.
family.properties: Pays on invariant-rich code — engines, parsers, compilers, state machines — not on glue. Validated on a first real project.
family.grade: A surviving mutant is a weak test to fix or dead code to delete — read each one before trusting the score. Validated on a first real project.
family.guard: Source properties from the design, not the code, and have a second pass try to beat them. Guards the blind spot — a test that just restates the code — that mutation grading cannot see.
family.behavior-lock-rules: Characterize before change, reproduce by the means the system admits, and hold characterization tests to the same adequacy bar.
family.constitution-rules: The governing-law rules: tools are authoritative, and the deadlock protocol for when tool rules and human constraints collide.
family.verb-skills: The procedures for running the work itself: attention-sizing units, de-risking the plan, sizing PRs to the operator, root-causing repeat failures, and reporting the decisions made.
family.map-recovery: Recover what the code actually does — by reading it, not trusting names — and write it down: the footgun register of name-vs-behavior traps, the silent-failure census, and the dated System Truth Atlas that assembles them with a ranked risk register. The recovered spec every later move reads from.
rule.c1:
Statement: after any edit targeting a constraint (lint, types, complexity, size), run the checker before proceeding; never estimate compliance by reading the code.

Why: mental estimation of line widths, complexity, and type-correctness is unreliable and wastes context. Tools produce ground truth in one command.
rule.c2:
Statement: a constraint deadlock is confirmed when fixing A reliably re-breaks B (one round-trip is enough). Stop iterating; suppress the tool-enforced rule on the minimum lines with an inline reason — never a human-authored constraint — and log it.

Why: tool rules are heuristics with false positives; authored constraints are deliberate decisions. Suppress the heuristic, preserve the decision, keep it auditable.
rule.a1:
Statement: for any predicate x op limit (<, <=, >, >=), the suite must contain a case at x == limit — not only values to either side.

Why: spec wording like 'exceeds' or 'above' is ambiguous between > and >=. Without the equality case, both interpretations pass every 'strictly above' assertion and the wrong operator ships.
rule.a2:
Statement: any method that mutates state conditionally (cleanup, evict, prune, expire, gc, compact) must have a test where the trigger is NOT met and post-state == pre-state.

Why: re-implementors write the 'do the work' branch and omit the guard, producing a method that mutates every call. The no-op test is cheap, always required, almost always missing.
rule.a3:
Statement: test behavior at the edges of an object's lifecycle (after close/shutdown), ensure every parameter changes observable behavior (no silently-ignored params), and when units share a resource pin who owns it.

Why: per-unit surface contracts prove a method exists and runs the happy path; they miss the lifecycle state machine and cross-unit resource ownership — where 'both green, neither composes' bugs live.
rule.a4:
Statement: assert enum/literal-typed outputs by value (==), not by membership; every declared member must be reachable and produced by at least one test.

Why: assert x in {members} just restates the code — it passes for a constant return. Behavior is only pinned when each member is produced and checked; unreachable members are dead or missing branches.
rule.a5:
Statement: a test may declare an invariant only if it actually exercises it (a cross-process claim needs a cross-process test; a determinism claim needs varied inputs plus an equality assertion).

Why: a declared-but-unexercised invariant is a false guarantee — it reads as proof while proving nothing.
rule.a6:
Statement: for code returning a decision/metric over a structured input domain (parser, validator, classifier, rule engine), examples are insufficient. Require (a) a metamorphic/invariance property over the input shape, (b) a satisfiability audit (one impl can pass every case), and prefer (c) a reference oracle + generated corpus.

Why: the build loop builds exactly to the contract; example cases only constrain the shapes the author thought to enumerate, so nested/compound shapes ship wrong. This is PDD's core moat.
rule.a7:
Statement: every suite carries a golden case per behavior, one case per declared failure mode, and one per documented edge; golden-only suites are rejected.

Why: an undocumented failure mode is a design defect to escalate, not a test to silently add. Deriving negatives/edges from the spec keeps coverage traceable.
rule.a8:
Statement: mock only at external system boundaries (third-party API, network, external DB); never at internal seams. Each stub models the minimum behavior its case needs — no catch-all success stubs.

Why: mocking an internal layer tests the mock, not the composition. A catch-all stub makes every test pass regardless of the code under test.
rule.a9:
Statement: when building from a stated spec, every requirement maps to at least one test that would fail if the requirement were violated, through a mechanically checkable link — a shared id the test names, not a claim of coverage. A requirement with no such test is unverified; a test that maps to no requirement is scope drift or an unstated requirement.

Why: a green suite is silent about a requirement that has no test at all — green suite, unmet requirement, invisible absence — and line coverage cannot catch it because it counts lines, not requirements. This is the design-to-test direction of provenance, the dual of GD1 (every property cites a design source); run both and nothing in the spec ships untested and nothing in the suite is sourced from the code it tests. PROVISIONAL pending real-project proof.
rule.n1:
Statement: every error is either handled (recovered to a known-good state with an observable trace) or propagated to a caller/boundary that surfaces it. Ignoring a failure-signaling return value is neither.

Why: silent failures are the dominant agentic failure mode — a system that produces wrong answers confidently and can't be debugged. Logging is observation, not handling.
rule.n2:
Statement: name the specific exception(s) you handle; a broad catch is allowed only at a process/task boundary that is itself responsible for surfacing the failure.

Why: catching the wrong exception hides unrelated failures. Naming the exception declares the contract.
rule.n3:
Statement: a caught error is re-raised, wrapped-and-re-raised, or fully resolved with the resolution recorded in observable output.

Why: 'fully resolved' is the abused option — code 'handles' the error but writes nothing observable. If you can't point to where it's visible, it was swallowed.
rule.n4:
Statement: every spawned task/job has a documented completion observer (awaited, collected, callback, or error handler).

Why: an orphan task's failure leaves no trace — the async equivalent of a bare except: pass.
rule.n5:
Statement: every subprocess invocation checks the exit code and surfaces stderr on failure.

Why: a discarded non-zero exit is a silent failure one layer out — a failed subprocess becomes indistinguishable from a successful one.
rule.n6:
Statement: no test pattern that converts a framework/collection/import failure into a test-level pass (deferring a first-party import, mocking out a first-party module to dodge an import error, swallowing import errors).

Why: such tests run green while the implementation is broken — confidently-wrong CI verdicts, the exact failure NSF exists to kill.
rule.p1:
Statement: where behavior ranges over a large/structured input domain, assert invariants sampled by generators, not just enumerated examples.

Why: this operationalizes contract adequacy (A6) at scale — generators reach the shapes you didn't think to write.
rule.p2:
Statement: two property sources, one engine. Shape properties auto-derived from schemas (schema-valid, conservation, refs-resolve, deterministic, no-silent-failure); behavior properties hand-authored from design (conservation, round-trip, idempotence, monotonicity, determinism).

Why: schemas express shape cheaply; only design expresses relational behavior. PBT runs both; mutation grades both.
rule.g1:
Statement: a green suite is trusted only to the degree a mutation gate proves it actually catches bugs — this grades example tests AND properties. A property that kills no mutant is too weak; fix the property, never the threshold.

Why: coverage lies (high lines, low mutation). Mutation is the test for your tests. A first real-project pilot bore this out — and surfaced the refinement in Triage survivors: a survivor is a weak test or dead code, and the count misleads until you read each one.
rule.g2: Statement: a surviving mutant is one of two things — a weak test (the behavior is real but unpinned; fix it) or dead code (the mutated line can't change any output; delete it). Read and classify each survivor before concluding the test is at fault. Why it matters: the count lies on its own — on an already-hardened subsystem most survivors are dead/inert code no test can kill (one pilot module had 16 of 17 survivors dead, not missing tests), so triage turns the score into an action: write a property, or delete the code.
rule.gd1:
Statement: every property cites its design source ([constraint]/[decision]/footgun). A property authored from the implementation just restates it (proving nothing) and is forbidden; an unsourced one is a 'doubt', not a gate.

Why: a property that mirrors the code passes even when the code is wrong — mutation can't see a test that just restates the code. Provenance is the guard.
rule.gd2:
Statement: a second agent attempts a wrong implementation that still passes every property; a surviving wrong-impl reveals a missing property (the completeness-critic).

Why: this catches the one thing mutation can't — properties that are honest but incomplete. It is the mechanism PDD was founded on. PROVISIONAL.
rule.gd3:
Statement: a counterexample halts the run; classify it as bug / wrong-property / spec-gap; never auto-relax a property to make it pass.

Why: silently weakening a property to go green destroys the gate. Property-specific root-cause discipline.
rule.b1:
Statement: pin current behavior with tests before touching the code; those characterization tests are the recovered spec.

Why: in brownfield the code is the only source of truth — you must capture what it does before you can prove a change preserved it.
rule.b2:
Statement: prove a change didn't alter behavior by the strongest means the system admits — replay/diff if deterministic; golden-master, snapshot, metamorphic, or injected seams (clock/RNG/IO) if not.

Why: determinism is the easy mode, not an assumption. Where it doesn't hold, making the system testably reproducible is itself a remediation deliverable.
rule.b3:
Statement: the adequacy gates apply to characterization tests too.

Why: an example-shaped characterization test locks nothing — it lets regressions through the same way example-shaped contracts do.
rule.m1:
Statement: every place a name, type, or signature contradicts verified runtime behavior is captured as a footgun — the misleading appearance, the verified reality, a file:line anchor, and a last-verified date — in a committed register.

Why: a name-lie recovered by reading is expensive knowledge; left unrecorded it is re-discovered by the next person, or silently re-broken by the next edit the misleading name invites. A footgun is also an invariant in disguise — a constraint a later characterization test can lock.
rule.m2:
Statement: a MAP includes a census of silent-failure sites — swallowed exceptions, ignored failure returns, unwatched tasks, unchecked subprocess exits — each located (file:line) and classified real-risk vs benign by reading it.

Why: a suite can be all-green while the system is broken, because the error happened but left no trace. The census is the visibility half of the recovered map; it runs the No-Silent-Failure rules (N1–N6) as a discovery sweep before they become gates.
rule.m3:
Statement: the output of MAP is one committed, dated, anchored diagnostic in four parts — the verified code map, the footgun register, a ranked risk register (P(wrong) × cost, the de-risk lens turned on recovered-system risk), and the silent-failure census. Scope it to the critical subsystems, not the whole tree at uniform depth.

Why: brownfield work has no trustworthy ground truth to start from — names lie, tests may not constrain, failures may hide. The atlas is that ground truth, recovered deliberately rather than assumed; everything downstream (PIN, CARVE, PROVE) reads from it.
rule.v1:
Statement: recover ground truth by reading the system; the names lie. Produce a footgun atlas of where behavior contradicts naming.

Why: in this class of codebase, symbol and parameter names mislead. A grep hit or a type is a lead, not a fact — open and read the actual code.
rule.v2:
Statement: size every work unit to ~1 minute of operator planning + ~1 minute of completion review; carve on concerns, not lines or files.

Why: implementation is cheap; expert attention is the bottleneck. A unit that doesn't fit one attention episode can't be reviewed honestly.
rule.v3:
Statement: before committing a plan, prove the riskiest architectural assumption with a thin end-to-end walking skeleton that exercises the real thing; emit a ranked risk register + GREEN/RED/OVERRIDE.

Why: its value is temporal — it only retires a risk if it runs before the construction that assumes it. Retroactive de-risking is skipping.
rule.v4:
Statement: size PRs to the operator's attention/milestone, not the agent's logical change. Two no-engagement 'lgtm' cycles signal the unit is wrong.

Why: the agent optimizes locally (one logical change) and the operator pays globally (review fatigue). A smooth lgtm stream means the agent isn't earning each review.
rule.v5:
Statement: when 3+ reruns die on different symptoms without converging, stop patching and audit the upstream artifact for the single defect producing all of them; name it in one sentence and land a catcher.

Why: superficially-different failures often share one upstream cause; treating them as N bugs yields N patches and no convergence.
rule.v6:
Statement: run the full $0 deterministic check suite before any costly or irreversible step.

Why: every gate that catches a defect at run-time catches it identically at zero cost — the only difference is whether you discover it before or after spending.
rule.v7:
Statement: close every change with a report of the judgment calls, tradeoffs, and suppressions made — not a recap of what was done.

Why: implementation agents make decisions that are invisible unless surfaced. The report exists so the operator can accept, override, or escalate each one.
rule.v8: Statement: before building on a critical decision (one whose reversal forces rework beyond the local change), state an observable condition that would prove it wrong, wire a detector to watch for it, and produce proof proportionate to how much could break and how hard it is to undo — a named fallback at the cheap/reversible end, a de-risk spike at the cross-cutting/irreversible end. Why: a decision with no falsifying condition reads the same whether right or wrong — the test-adequacy gap moved up to the decision. It generalises the de-risk gate (V3) and adds the cheaper tiers. PROVISIONAL pending real-project proof.
rule.v9: Statement: before committing a design, surface the critical assumptions it rests on — the beliefs about environment, dependencies, behavior, timing, and scale that were never consciously chosen — and record each in a committed register with a falsifying condition (breaks-if), a consequence-anchored fragility, and a detector that watches for the break. High-fragility entries route to the de-risk spike (V3) or, if the assumption is really an ungated choice, the decision gate (V8); the rest carry a recorded fallback. Why: an assumption is the decision gate's blind spot widened — a decision is a choice you know you made and can gate; an assumption is a belief you did not know you held, so nothing gates it, yet it reads the same whether it holds or not. The surfacing is judgment (no checker lists what you assumed without noticing); PDD's contribution is forcing each one into a falsifiable, detectable, consequence-sized structure and checking that structure mechanically — not scoring assumptions with a stochastic panel. PROVISIONAL pending real-project proof.
rule.v10:
Statement: a critical spec term carries exactly one meaning, pinned once. A term a reader could take two ways (ambiguity — two clear meanings — not vagueness, which is no clear meaning) splits the spec silently: each reader is sure they understood, and they build different things. Define each critical term once as a checkable condition, or split it into two named terms.

Why: it is the spec-level form of the footgun (a name that lies), caught before code; the most dangerous term is the one both readers felt sure they understood. The judgment (which terms are critical, whether two uses differ) is human; a one-definition glossary is the checkable structure. PROVISIONAL pending real-project proof.
rule.v11:
Statement: many operations come in pairs — create/delete, open/close, acquire/release, encode/decode, subscribe/unsubscribe, authenticate/authorize. When one half is present, the other must be present, or explicitly marked absent-by-design with a reason. A silently missing inverse is the dangerous direction: the resource created but never freed, the token issued but never revoked, the cache filled but never invalidated.

Why: this catches an incompleteness that requirement-by-requirement review misses, because the missing half was never written down to review — the structure implies it (five add_* and no remove_* is louder than any one requirement). Mostly mechanical in a naming-disciplined codebase: grep each present half for its partner. PROVISIONAL pending real-project proof.
rule.v12:
Statement: before code, each behavior in the design names its contract (inputs, outputs, invariants), its failure modes (what goes wrong and the response), and its edges (empty, zero, max, boundary). A happy-path-only design defers the hard half to implementation, where it is decided by accident; a present-but-empty section (TBD/TODO) counts as missing — it reads as done while specifying nothing.

Why: the expensive errors come from the design that never said what happens when the input is empty or the dependency is down, and absence has no anchor in review. It is upstream of the verification rules — trace-to-tests can only trace requirements that exist, and cover-negatives-and-edges can only cover failure modes the design named. PROVISIONAL pending real-project proof.
actor.operator: The human owner. Holds the sign-off gates, sets the Constitution, and is the one whose attention the whole methodology is budgeted around.
actor.builder: The strong-model agent that does the work across MAP, PIN, CARVE, and SHIP — one capable model, not a cheap-implementer loop.
actor.auditor: The independent verifier — agent plus CI gates — that checks at SHIP and PROVE.
failure.adequacy-gap: Tests pass but don't pin behavior — the spec/contract was incomplete in ways no reviewer can see. Closed by the Adequacy gates + Properties + mutation grading.
failure.visibility-gap: Code runs but errors hid — silent failures that make a wrong system look healthy and undebuggable. Closed by No-Silent-Failures.
failure.behavior-drift: A change silently altered behavior. Closed by Behavior Lock (characterize + reproduce + prove).
failure.tautological-property: A property that mirrors the implementation passes even when the implementation is wrong — mutation can't see it. Closed by the Guard layer (provenance + refutation + triage).
failure.architectural-risk: A critical assumption proves wrong after the project is already built on it, forcing re-architecting. Closed by the De-risk gate (prove it before you commit).
failure.untrusted-truth: Names, types, schemas, and inherited docs contradict what the code actually does, so any claim built on them is a guess that reads as fact — the brownfield starting condition. Closed by MAP recovery: read the system, register the footguns, census the silent failures, assemble the atlas.
